author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
The Whole Discussion is About Grace.
title: The Whole Discussion is About Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Let us, however, observe what our author answers, after laying before himself the question wherein he seems indeed so intolerable to Christian hearts.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He says: “But you will tell me this is what disturbs a great many,—that you do not maintain that it is by the grace of God that a man is able to be without sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Certainly this is what causes us disturbance; this is what we object to him.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He touches the very point of the case.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: This is what causes us such utter pain to endure it; this is why we cannot bear to have such points debated by Christians, owing to the love which we feel towards others and towards themselves.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Well, let us hear how he clears himself from the objectionable character of the question he has raised.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “What blindness of ignorance,” he exclaims, “what sluggishness of an uninstructed mind, which supposes that that is maintained and held to be without God’s grace which it only hears ought to be attributed to God!”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now, if we knew nothing of what follows this outburst of his, and formed our opinion on simply hearing these words, we might suppose that we had been led to a wrong view of our opponents by the spread of report and by the asseveration of some suitable witnesses among the brethren.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For how could it have been more pointedly and truly stated that the possibility of not sinning, to whatever extent it exists or shall exist in man, ought only to be attributed to God?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: This too is our own affirmation.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
